title: Dog Excessive Tearing and Brown Staining: Causes & Triage
meta_desc: Discover why dogs get brown tear stains, when it becomes a medical concern, and how photos help your vet diagnose the cause of excessive tearing.
content_html:
TL;DR: Excessive tearing in dogs, or epiphora, causes reddish-brown staining that is usually a non-emergency cosmetic issue unless accompanied by eye pain, redness, or yellow-green discharge.

What is excessive tearing in dogs and why does it cause brown fur stains?

Excessive tearing, medically known as epiphora, occurs when your dog's eyes produce too many tears or if the tear ducts cannot drain them properly. The characteristic brown or reddish staining you see on the fur is caused by porphyrins. These are iron-rich molecules produced when the body breaks down red blood cells, which are then excreted through tears, saliva, and urine. When they sit on the fur and react with light and air, they oxidize and change color.

Is my dog's excessive eye tearing or staining an emergency?

Urgency Level: Low. In most cases, brown staining is a chronic, non-emergency cosmetic issue. However, you should monitor for these signs and take the following actions:

  • Schedule a routine veterinary visit to rule out underlying irritation like allergies, ingrown eyelashes, or blocked tear ducts.
  • Seek prompt veterinary care if your dog begins pawing at their eyes.
  • Contact a vet immediately if you notice significant redness or if the discharge turns yellow or green.

How does providing a photo help triage my dog's eye issues?

Sharing a clear, well-lit photo of your dog's face can be incredibly helpful for a triage professional by allowing them to:

  • Identify the exact pattern and spread of the staining.
  • Check the condition of the skin underneath the wet fur for irritation or infection.
  • See whether the eye itself looks clear, cloudy, or inflamed.
  • Determine if the issue is a simple drainage problem or something requiring more immediate medical attention.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Imperforate lacrimal puncta can be an infrequent cause of epiphora in young dogs. Obstructions of the nasolacrimal duct may lead to mucopurulent discharge from the lacrimal puncta. Radiographs of the skull after injection of contrast material into the duct (dacryocystorhinography) may be necessary to establish the site, cause, and prognosis of chronic obstructions. Treatment focuses on maintaining the patency of the duct with topical antibiotic solutions, and temporary catheterization with tubing or monofilament nylon suture may be necessary to maintain patency during healing. In cases with irreversible damage to the nasolacrimal apparatus, surgical creation of a new drainage pathway (conjunctivorhinostomy or conjunctivoralostomy) can be performed to empty tears into the nasal cavity, sinus, or mouth.

Chapter: Ophthalmology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 490)

faqs.question: Can I clean the stains with regular water?
faqs.answer: Yes, wiping the area daily with a clean, damp cloth can help reduce the buildup of porphyrins and prevent the stains from darkening.

faqs.question: Is tear staining painful for my dog?
faqs.answer: Tear staining itself is not painful, but the underlying cause—such as an eye infection or an eyelash rubbing against the cornea—might cause discomfort.

faqs.question: Do certain foods cause more staining?
faqs.answer: While some owners report changes after switching diets, there is limited scientific evidence linking specific ingredients to tear production. Consult your vet before making major dietary changes.
